@graph.name: Mercedes Vito CDI Engine Era Map

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes Vito CDI engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned OM601 engine typically costs £1,500–£4,000 supply only; the OM611 costs £2,000–£5,000; and the OM646 costs £2,500–£5,500. A used OM601 engine can be found for £900–£2,500. A reconditioned engine (fully cleaned, tested, parts replaced) typically costs £1,200–£2,200, with a total installed cost around £1,800–£2,800. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,500. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your Vito.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes Vito CDI engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends which engine your Vito has. The OM601 (W638, 1996–2003) is a robust, durable indirect‑injection diesel — one of Mercedes' most reliable engines. The OM611 (1999–2006) introduced common‑rail injection but suffers from injector seal failure ('Black Death'), EGR and turbo actuator issues. The OM646 (2006–2010) is considered one of the best Mercedes diesel engines — with proper maintenance, examples exceed 500,000 miles. However, injector seal failure remains a significant issue. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Vito CDI engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: OM601 (W638): cooling system failure, timing chain wear, fuel injection system issues, oil leaks. OM611/OM646 (CDI): injector seal failure ('Black Death'), EGR valve clogging, turbocharger actuator failure, injector issues. The OM646 is more reliable than the OM611 but shares the injector seal weakness.

mainEntity.name: Does the Vito CDI have a timing belt or chain?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM601, OM611 and OM646 all use timing chains. The OM646 uses a duplex chain at the front of the engine, which is more robust than the simplex chain of later OM651 engines.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Vito CDI engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and overall condition. A Vito CDI is a £2,000–£10,000 vehicle — a £2,500–£3,500 recon is 25–175% of the car's value. For lower‑value examples (sub‑£3,000), replacement may exceed our 70% Mercedes scrap threshold. A well‑maintained, lower‑mileage example with the OM646 engine justifies replacement; a tired, high‑mileage example may not.

mainEntity.name: What is 'Black Death' and how do I prevent it?

acceptedAnswer.text: 'Black Death' refers to injector seal failure, where copper sealing rings under the injectors degrade, allowing unburnt diesel and exhaust gases to escape and create hard carbon deposits around the injectors. Prevention: regular service with high‑quality diesel, and having the injector seals inspected and replaced at the first sign of chuffing or diesel smell. Many specialists recommend removing and re‑sealing all injectors as a preventative measure on high‑mileage examples.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes Vito CDI Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the range across all generations; specific costs vary by era — see the Era Map above. Used OM601 engines are typically the most affordable; OM646 units command a premium due to their reputation for longevity.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Vito CDI engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The OM601 and OM611 are relatively straightforward to work on; the OM646 requires more specialised knowledge.

cta.label: → Compare Vito CDI engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Injector Seal Failure — "Black Death" (OM611 & OM646 — All Years)
problems.symptoms: Ticking or chuffing noise from the injector area, black carbon buildup around injectors, rough idle, loss of power, diesel smell in the cabin, engine warning light.
problems.repairCost: £300–£800 (injector seal replacement, plus cleaning)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you hear chuffing or smell diesel

problems.recommendation: Injector seal failure — known as "Black Death" — is the most common issue across OM611 and OM646 CDI engines. The copper sealing rings under the injectors degrade, allowing unburnt diesel and exhaust gases to escape, creating hard, tar‑like carbon deposits around the injectors. If left unaddressed, the carbon can damage the cylinder head and make injector removal extremely difficult. Many specialists recommend removing and re‑sealing all injectors as a preventative measure, especially on high‑mileage examples.

problems.title: EGR Valve & Turbocharger Actuator Issues (OM611 & OM646 — All Years)
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, limp mode, black smoke, poor throttle response, engine warning light (P0299 underboost).
problems.repairCost: £300–£800 (EGR cleaning or replacement); £400–£1,200 (turbo actuator replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught before turbo damage)
problems.recommendation: EGR valve clogging and turbocharger actuator failure are common on OM611 and OM646 engines. The EGR valve becomes blocked with carbon deposits, causing loss of power and limp mode. The turbo actuator can also fail, leading to underboost and limp mode. Regular maintenance — including EGR cleaning — is essential. At the first sign of loss of power or limp mode, have the EGR and turbo system inspected.

problems.title: Cooling System & Timing Chain Wear (OM601 — W638 1996–2003)
problems.symptoms: Overheating, coolant loss, temperature gauge fluctuations (cooling system); rattling noise on cold start, metallic ticking (timing chain).
problems.repairCost: £300–£800 (cooling system refresh); £800–£2,000 (timing chain and tensioner replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught before catastrophic failure)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if overheating or rattling occurs

problems.recommendation: The OM601 is a robust engine, but the cooling system — particularly the water pump and radiator — can fail with age. Timing chain wear is also a known issue on higher‑mileage examples. If you notice temperature fluctuations, have the cooling system inspected immediately. At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, have the timing chain and tensioner checked.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Vito CDI — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch it early — before it damages the cylinder head
Catch it early — before chain snaps
Little left to salvage — replacement is the only option

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Vito CDI? Check These First
buyIf: * OM601 (W638): cooling system refreshed — no overheating or rattle
* OM611/OM646 (W638/W639): injector seals replaced or inspected — no "Black Death"
* Full Mercedes service history with regular oil changes
* OM646: 2006+ model with duplex timing chain — considered the most reliable
avoidIf: * OM611/OM646: chuffing noise or diesel smell — "Black Death" injector seal failure — walk away
* OM601: overheating history or cold‑start rattle — cooling or timing chain issues
* Poor service history — all these engines are sensitive to neglect
* OM611/OM646: loss of power with no diagnosis — EGR or turbo issues likely

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Vito CDI range spans three engine families across two generations. The OM601 (W638, 1996–2003) is the most robust — a simple, durable indirect‑injection diesel — but cooling system and timing chain wear are the main watchpoints. The OM611 (1999–2006) introduced common‑rail injection but suffers from injector seal failure ("Black Death"), EGR and turbo actuator issues. The OM646 (2006–2010) is considered one of the best Mercedes diesel engines — with proper maintenance, examples exceed 500,000 miles — but injector seal failure remains a significant issue. Buy on full service history, check for "Black Death" on CDI engines, and never ignore a loss of power or rough idle.

cta.label: → Compare Vito CDI engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes Vito CDI (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Injector Area (OM611/OM646) — Check for black carbon buildup around injectors; "Black Death" indicator
* Chuffing / Diesel Smell — Listen for ticking or chuffing; injector seal failure indicator
* Loss of Power / Limp Mode — Check for underboost; EGR or turbo actuator indicator
* Service History — including which generation (W638 or W639) and confirmed oil‑change intervals
* Cooling System (OM601) — Check for temperature fluctuations; cooling system failure indicator
* Timing Chain — Listen for cold‑start rattle; timing chain wear indicator
* Turbo Health — Listen for whistling or whining; turbo failure indicator
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures
